schools.summary: Mark deliberately titles Christ 'Son of God' rather than 'Son of man' (Matthew) to lead the reader from the human to the divine; 'beginning' marks the opening of the Gospel dispensation through John.
'Beginning' may serve as a superscription for the whole Gospel or for the Baptist's ministry specifically; 'Gospel' transitions from its oral to its written sense.
The Gospel was not new in substance—preached to Abraham and in Eden—but John marks the end of the Law and the public unveiling of the Gospel of the kingdom.
The Baptist's preaching and baptism constitute the 'beginning' of the Gospel narrative proper, distinguished from the Gospel's eternal purpose.

debates.question: Does 'beginning' serve as a title for the whole book or refer only to John's ministry?

sides.position: A superscription for the entire Gospel
Refers specifically to John's preaching as the opening of the Gospel era

outliers.note: Presses the distinction between Christ's Sonship and ours: he is Son by origin, truth, and birth—not by adoption or name as we are.

schools.summary: Mark properly begins with prophetic testimony; the compound citation from Malachi and Isaiah anchors the Gospel in the unified witness of the Old Testament.
Pseudo-Chrysostom explains the combined quotation as a compression of two distinct prophecies; Theophylact reads 'my angel' as pointing to John's angelic mode of life.
The textual question—'prophets' vs. 'Isaiah'—is carefully weighed; Robertson and others note that citing Isaiah as chief prophet when combining OT texts was standard practice.

debates.question: Does the original text read 'in the prophets' or 'in Isaiah the prophet'?

sides.position: 'In Isaiah the prophet' is original; Isaiah named as chief because the climactic citation is his
'In the prophets' is original or preferred because two prophets are actually quoted

outliers.note: Reads 'my messenger' as a double fulfillment: John is called 'angel' because he lived an angelic life and walked before Christ as the closest of companions.

schools.summary: Isaiah 40:3 is read as a highway prepared for God's coming to his people; John's wilderness voice fulfills this image.
Cyril connects the wilderness cry to baptism as the end of the Old Testament era and the beginning of the new.
John's baptism was preparatory, not salvific: it preached the way to remission but could not itself grant it, since actual remission requires Christ's baptism.
Theophylact and Pseudo-Chrysostom distinguish carefully: John's rite brought men to repentance; only Christ's baptism actually remits sins through the Holy Spirit.

Robertson and Clarke note that John's epochal coming (ἐγένετο, not ἦν) marks a genuine new beginning; Clarke derives 'John' from Yehochanan, 'Yahweh's grace,' fitting for the herald of the Gospel of grace.
Wesley reads 'the baptism of repentance' as preaching repentance accompanied by baptism as its sign and means.

debates.question: Did John's baptism actually remit sins or only point to the remission found in Christ?

sides.position: John's baptism preached toward remission but could not itself grant it
The phrase means John preached repentance with baptism as its accompanying sign

outliers.note: Reads the locusts in John's diet as symbolic of the Jews who leaped up but fell back to earth, and the wild honey as the sweetness of the Gentiles drawn to Christ.

schools.summary: The imperfect tenses (Robertson) describe a steady stream of people coming to John; Clarke notes the Jewish proselyte-baptism custom required solemn renunciation of idolatry beforehand, making John's call to confession all the more pointed.
Cyril sees Jerusalem's coming to John as confirmation of the city's prerogative for all things good, and the public confession of sins as preparation for redemption.

John's clothing is rough cloth woven of camel's hair (not camel-skin), typical of penitents and men of austere lives; his diet of locusts and wild honey echoes Elijah's severity. Robertson and Ellicott note the Elijah typology implicit in Mark's description.
Gill adds Talmudic and patristic context: the dress of the desert penitent was the recognised uniform of the prophetic calling.

outliers.note: Cites Talmudic passages on camel's hair garments in detail, confirming the historical credibility of Mark's description and showing its currency in Jewish penitential practice.

schools.summary: 'I am not worthy to stoop down and loose his sandal-latchet'—a slave's task, the lowest service. Ellicott notes Mark's verb ('stoop down and unloose') is more physically vivid than Matthew's 'carry'; the genuine humility of John before Christ is underscored by the grammarians as sincere, not rhetorical.
Gill stresses that John's preaching centred on the dignity of Christ's person, the excellency of his office, and the nature of his work.

The contrast between water and Spirit baptism is clear in every synoptic: John's rite is a symbol of the spiritual reality Christ alone administers. Ellicott observes that Mark omits Matthew's 'and fire,' possibly because that image would be less intelligible to Gentile readers.
Clarke insists water baptism is not to be rested in: it is only an emblem pointing to the baptism of the Holy Spirit, which Christ alone can give.
Cyril ties John's Spirit-filled life (from the womb) to his role: sanctified precisely so he might baptize the Lord, though he could not himself give the Spirit.

Bede: Christ's baptism showed approval of John's rite and, through the dove's descent, sanctified the waters of Jordan for future believers. Augustine notes that Matthew's 'This is my Son' and Mark/Luke's 'Thou art my Son' are both true: the heavenly voice declared his Sonship to those present and to him directly.

Pseudo-Chrysostom reasons that Christ was not baptized for remission of sins (he had none) but to show by the nature of his baptism that he was ordaining a new rite, standing between the Jewish and Christian forms.
Robertson marks the 'rent asunder' (σχιζομένους) as more vivid than Matthew's 'opened,' typical of Mark's bold language; he notes Mark's characteristic εὐθύς ('straightway') appears here.

debates.question: Why did Christ submit to John's baptism?

sides.position: To sanctify the waters, approve John's ministry, and model baptism for believers
To inaugurate a transition between the old rite and the new Christian baptism, not for remission

schools.summary: Ellicott highlights Mark's σχιζομένους ('rent open') as more violent and vivid than Matthew or Luke's language of 'opening'—typical of Mark's boldly dramatic style. Gill clarifies it was Jesus who came up out of the water, not John.

Robertson notes the difference between Mark/Luke's direct address ('Thou art my beloved Son') and Matthew's testimony to bystanders ('This is my Son'): both report the same voice differently because different hearers received it differently. The echo of Psalm 2:7 and Isaiah 42:1 is heard across the schools.
Cyril draws the lesson: the Father was well pleased in the Son; unless we too are well pleased in him, we have no life.

Chrysostom reads the Spirit's leading into the wilderness as a model for every baptized person: post-baptism temptation is not contrary to expectation but is the Spirit's way of preparing the soldier of Christ.
Bede insists it was the Holy Spirit who led him—not the evil spirit—and cites Luke's 'full of the Spirit' as confirmation; Christ went willingly under divine direction.

Robertson underscores ἐκβάλλει ('driveth out')—the same verb Mark uses for casting out demons—as more forceful than Matthew's 'led up' or Luke's 'led'; it does not imply compulsion against the will but the Spirit's energetic impulse. Wesley draws the general principle: extraordinary divine favor is regularly followed by extraordinary temptation.

Mark's distinctive detail—'with the wild beasts'—is absent from Matthew and Luke. Clarke notes the savage wilderness setting amplified the horror of the scene; Ellicott identifies the beasts (panther, bear, wolf, hyena, jackal) that inhabited the Judean desert.
Wesley observes that the wild beasts had no power to harm Christ, and that Mark adds valuable particulars not found in the other Gospels.

outliers.note: Reads Christ among the wild beasts as an Adam typology: as Adam before the Fall had peaceable dominion over the animals, so the second Adam, unfallen and sinless, restores that dominion—the beasts obey.

schools.summary: Bede reads the sequence typologically: 'when the Law ceases, the Gospel arises in its steps.' John imprisoned = the Law ending its work in Judea; Jesus preaching in Galilee = the Gospel going out to the wider world. Pseudo-Jerome extends this to the Pauline mission: John in prison, Law in Judaea; Jesus in Galilee, Paul among the Gentiles.

Pseudo-Chrysostom sees Christ's withdrawal to Galilee after John's arrest as a practical lesson in prudence: he did not resist violent men but preserved himself for the cross, teaching believers not to court persecution.

Robertson and Ellicott note that Mark uses John's imprisonment as the starting point for Jesus' Galilean ministry, consistent with Peter's preaching pattern; what is assumed here is explained in Mark 6:17.
Origen reflects on the distinction and identity of 'the kingdom of God' and 'the kingdom of heaven'—essentially the same reality, differing in emphasis.

Ellicott notes that 'the time is fulfilled' echoes Paul's 'fullness of time' (Galatians 4:4; Ephesians 1:10); Clarke specifies that Daniel 9:24–27 fixed the precise moment. 'Believe the gospel' is unique to Mark's version.
Wesley hears Christ announcing the specific time foretold by Daniel, now fully arrived.
Gill catalogues the possible referents of 'the time'—the end of the Mosaic dispensation, the fulness fixed by divine appointment, the era predicted by the prophets.

Bede notes that God chose fishermen and unlettered men so that faith might rest in divine power rather than eloquence; their immediate obedience ('straightway they left their nets') is the paradigm of radical discipleship.

Theophylact explains that Peter and Andrew had already been disciples of John the Baptist, and after John's testimony to Jesus they had followed him briefly; now, grieving John's imprisonment, they had returned to fishing—making this call a return to what they had briefly tasted.
Robertson notes Mark's doubled παρά ('along beside') makes the scene vivid; 'casting a net' (ἀμφιβάλλοντας) describes the side-to-side sweeping motion of a circular cast-net.

outliers.note: Draws a pointed contrast: when fish are caught in a net they die, but when men are caught by the net of holy preaching they are made alive—the Gospel's fishing produces life, not death.

schools.summary: Robertson highlights Mark's addition of γενέσθαι ('become'): making fishers of men is not instant but a slow process—Christ undertakes to transform men of a trade into proclaimers of the kingdom. He can, and he will.
Gill notes the call to 'come after me' as a call to leave worldly employment and become disciples—a total reorientation of vocation.
Wesley's brevity is striking: 'Happy they who follow Christ at the first call!' The instant abandonment of the nets is the model of evangelical response.
Gill notes that Peter and Andrew left their nets—which were perhaps their entire livelihood—without delay.

Robertson notes 'a little further' (ὀλίγον) as a characteristic Marcan detail; the brothers James and John were mending their nets (καταρτίζοντας)—preparing for the next catch, diligent in their work when the call came.
Gill records the location as still by the sea side, emphasizing the spatial continuity of the two calls.

Ellicott notes the hired servants as detail unique to Mark, indicating Zebedee ran a substantial operation—the sons did not leave poverty but a thriving business. Robertson adds that the same co-operative arrangement with Simon and Andrew is confirmed in Luke 5:10.
Gill observes the call was immediate ('straightway') and that Christ had appointed these men long before to the service.

Bede draws the central contrast: the Scribes taught what Moses and the prophets wrote, but Jesus, as God and Lord of Moses, spoke either by adding to the Law or by altering it—on his own authority. The Sabbath setting is fitting: rest from sin, not merely from labour, is the Law's deeper intent.

Theophylact explains that Mark's narrative leaps forward 'not in the order of history but in the order of mystery,' opening with the first miracle on the Sabbath. Pseudo-Jerome reads the synagogue setting allegorically.

Robertson and Ellicott provide geographical and textual context for Capernaum and the synagogue setting; Barnes notes that the synagogue practice of inviting any competent person to address the congregation made Christ's teaching possible.

The crowd's astonishment rests on the contrast in manner: the Scribes cited authorities and tradition (Moses said, the elders said), whereas Christ spoke directly—'I say to you'—as the sovereign author of truth. Robertson notes the imperfect ἐξεπλήσσοντο describes a continuous state of amazement.
Calvin notes that the novelty which astonished the crowd lay not in doctrinal innovation but in the power and freedom of Christ's manner—a difference of spirit, not merely of style.

Augustine and Bede note the painful irony: the unclean spirit confesses what men, hearing the same teaching, fail to believe. The demon's presence in the synagogue—the place of worship—shows how deeply the enemy had infiltrated the people of God before Christ's arrival.

Theophylact reads 'What have we to do with thee?' (τί ἡμῖν καὶ σοί) as an admission of absolute opposition: demon and Christ share nothing in common. Pseudo-Chrysostom notes the demons used the plural because demonic possession involves a community of evil spirits.
Clarke and Robertson note the formula 'unclean spirit' (πνεῦμα ἀκάθαρτον) appears here for the first time in Mark—a characteristic designation in this Gospel.

The demon's cry 'I know thee who thou art—the Holy One of God' is not a confession of faith but a desperate bid to neutralize Christ by naming him (Lightfoot documents Jewish exorcism practice: to name the spirit, or the one adjuring, was to claim power over them). The demon inverts this convention—finding that naming Christ gains it nothing.
Gill notes that the demon's fear and knowledge differ entirely from saving faith: the devils believe and tremble (James 2:19).

outliers.note: Details the Jewish practice of adjurations using the name of the spirit or holy person; the demon's invocation of Christ's name follows this pattern but is entirely powerless against him.

schools.summary: Christ's command is terse and absolute: 'Hold thy peace' (φιμώθητι—'be muzzled') and 'come out of him.' No incantation, no prolonged ritual. Robertson and Clarke both note the contrast with the Jewish exorcists who required elaborate formulas; here authority is exercised by a word.
Gill notes the command silences the demon before expelling it—the testimony of unclean spirits is not sought by Christ.

The convulsion (σπαράξαν) and loud cry mark the demon's parting act of malice—it obeyed but not without a show of force. Robertson notes that Mark's vivid participles convey the physical drama absent from the parallel in Luke. Ellicott and Clarke observe the departure was complete.
Calvin finds Christ's authority made more visible precisely by the demon's violent resistance: the harder the enemy fights, the clearer the victory.

The crowd's question—'A new doctrine? And with authority he commands even unclean spirits, and they obey him'—ties teaching and miracle together. Robertson and Ellicott note that 'new' here means unprecedented in kind and power, not contrary to Moses. The witnesses debate among themselves: they recognise both dimensions of Christ's authority.
Calvin notes that 'new doctrine' does not imply departure from Moses but a new manner—the freedom and power of the divine speaker confirmed by the accompanying work.

Robertson notes the imperfect ἐξῆλθεν describes the news spreading outward, wave upon wave, through the whole surrounding region of Galilee. The report of Christ's authority—in word and over demons—preceded him wherever he went.
Gill marks the geographical scope: the fame spread through all the region round about Galilee, reaching well beyond Capernaum.

Bede and Pseudo-Jerome see the move from synagogue to house as part of Christ's pattern: public teaching followed by private healing—the grace of Christ reaches both the gathered congregation and the individual household.

Theophylact notes the characteristic 'straightway' (εὐθύς): after teaching and casting out the demon, Jesus immediately enters Peter's house—his mercy knows no rest. Pseudo-Chrysostom draws the lesson that Christ's work is uninterrupted and all-encompassing.

Robertson and Ellicott note that 'they' (Simon, Andrew, James, John) accompany Jesus—the four new disciples present from the outset of the private miracle. Clarke and Barnes cross-reference the parallel accounts in Matthew and Luke.

Gill records that Simon's mother-in-law lay in a great fever (Luke's clinical language), and the disciples immediately told Jesus about her—an act of intercession on her behalf, trusting his power to heal.

Gill notes that Christ took her by the hand, raised her up, and the fever instantly left—a complete and immediate restoration. Her subsequent service at table proves the healing was total: she did not convalesce but was as well as if she had never been sick.

Theophylact and Pseudo-Chrysostom note the crowd came at evening 'when the sun did set'—that is, after the Sabbath had ended—because the people, observing the Law, waited until they could lawfully carry the sick. The size of the crowd ('all the city') reflects the growing conviction that Christ could heal.
Bede draws a spiritual parallel: the setting sun also symbolizes the end of the Old Law under which the people waited; Christ heals at the onset of the new day's freedom.
Robertson and Ellicott note the imperfect tenses describing the stream of people: 'they were bringing' and 'they were gathering'—a sustained evening scene of many healings.

'All the city was gathered together at the door'—Robertson notes the hyperbole common to popular history: the whole city represents the mass movement of people drawn by news of the miraculous. Barnes and Gill both emphasise the universal scope of the healings that evening.
Wesley sees in the crowd's eagerness an implicit appeal to Christ's compassion—they came because they had nowhere else to turn.

'He healed many'—Robertson notes that Mark does not say 'all' as Matthew does; the 'many' here reflects the actual number rather than the totality (though Luke does say 'all who had any sick'). The casting out of many demons is Mark's characteristic detail.

Calvin explains the silence imposed on the demons: Christ would not accept testimony from unclean spirits, since their witness would contaminate the Gospel's credibility and lead hearers to associate him with the demonic. Clarke adds that impure lips are not fit to herald holy truth.
Wesley notes that the demons knew him—an involuntary acknowledgement of his Lordship that he deliberately silenced.

debates.question: Why did Christ forbid the demons to speak even when they spoke truth about him?

sides.position: Their testimony would taint the Gospel by associating it with demonic sources
He suppressed them to prevent premature public disclosure of his identity

schools.summary: Theophylact and Pseudo-Chrysostom see Christ's solitary early prayer as a double lesson: he withdraws from praise to avoid the crowd's admiration, and teaches us to pray in secret and alone. The darkness before dawn is the fitting hour of unbroken communion with the Father.
Augustine frames the mystery: Christ is himself God, yet as Mediator he prays as man—his prayer schools us in dependence on the Father even while he is the eternal Son.

Robertson notes 'very early in the morning, while it was yet night' (πρωῒ ἔννυχα λίαν)—an unusually precise temporal expression; the solitary place (ἔρημος τόπος) echoes the wilderness of the temptation. Mark's brevity here magnifies the significance of the act.

Robertson highlights κατεδίωξεν ('hunted him down')—a strong compound verb expressing the urgency with which Simon led the search. The plural 'Simon and they that were with him' marks Peter's instinctive leadership of the group.
Gill notes the motivation: the crowds were waiting and the disciples were anxious not to disappoint them.

'All men seek for thee'—a report of universal popular interest, still fresh after the previous evening's healings. The disciples, well-meaning, assume that widespread demand requires Christ's return to Capernaum; the next verse reveals his different understanding of his mission.
Gill reads the disciples' words as a natural but incomplete assessment: they equated popular approval with the goal of the mission.

'Let us go into the next towns'—Robertson and Lightfoot note that the Greek κωμοπόλεις ('market-towns') denotes nearby towns larger than villages; Christ is not fleeing success but following the divine schedule of the mission. 'For therefore came I forth' is deliberately ambiguous: it may refer to his departure from the Father or from Capernaum—the Fathers often read both layers.
Wesley and Clarke hear Christ's clear sense of divine appointment: he came forth to preach broadly, not to be the healer of one city.
Gill stresses the preaching priority: healing was a sign attending the word; the word itself was the primary errand.

A summary statement: Jesus preached in their synagogues throughout all Galilee and cast out demons—twin pillars of the Galilean ministry as Mark conceives it. Robertson notes the imperfect ἦν…κηρύσσων describes the continuous pattern, not a single event.
Gill marks the scope: all Galilee, all their synagogues—the mission saturates the region before narrowing to individuals.

Augustine and Bede dwell on the leper's exemplary petition: 'If thou wilt, thou canst.' He does not doubt Christ's power but humbly submits to his will—the model of faith in prayer. Bede notes that the leper worshipped first, then asked.
Chrysostom and Theophylact stress that the leper did not say 'If thou prayest to God, thou canst'—he addressed Christ directly as the source of power, a form of implicit worship of his divinity.

Robertson and Ellicott document the Mosaic law on leprosy (Leviticus 13:45)—the leper was required to cry 'Unclean! Unclean!' and live apart; his approach to Jesus thus already breaks the Law's quarantine, driven by desperate faith.

Robertson notes a textual variant: some MSS read ὀργισθείς ('being angry/indignant') where the majority read σπλαγχνισθείς ('moved with compassion'). Most commentators accept 'compassion' as the harder reading explaining the softer; the compassion—or indignation at the ravages of disease and sin—leads Christ to touch the untouchable. The touch itself is significant: Christ was not defiled by the leper; the leper was cleansed by him.
Gill stresses the divine prerogative: Christ could touch what the Law forbade human contact with, because his holiness is not diminished by contact with defilement—it flows outward and overcomes it.

debates.question: Does the best text read 'moved with compassion' or 'being angry/indignant'?

sides.position: 'Moved with compassion' (σπλαγχνισθείς) — the majority reading, depicting tender mercy
'Being indignant' (ὀργισθείς) — a minority reading possibly reflecting anger at disease or the man's presumption

schools.summary: The healing is immediate and total: 'As soon as he had spoken, immediately the leprosy departed from him, and he was cleansed.' Gill notes this is a work only God can do—instantaneous removal of a disease the Law could only manage by quarantine proves the power of the divine healer.

Robertson focuses on ἐμβριμησάμενος ('strictly charging')—a strong word elsewhere used of indignation or stern emotion; Ellicott notes Mark's typically vivid language. Clarke cross-references the Mosaic requirement for the healed leper to show himself to the priest (Leviticus 14:2).
Gill records the charge as emphatic: Christ dismissed the man with an urgency that underscores both the seriousness of the command to silence and the necessity of the priestly offering.

Robertson explains the Mosaic requirement at length: Leviticus 14:2–32 required the healed leper to present himself, bring offerings, and receive the priest's declaration. By insisting on this, Christ upholds the Law and provides the priestly establishment with an official, verifiable witness to the healing.

Calvin reads the command to silence as prudential: Christ wanted to prevent tumult and premature political misuse of his popular following. He honoured the Law in directing the man to the priest, showing he came not to abolish but to fulfil.
Wesley observes the purpose: 'for a testimony unto them'—the healing was to serve as public evidence, not a private secret, once the proper legal process was completed.

The man's disobedience—however motivated by gratitude—had a direct consequence: Jesus could no longer enter the cities openly but had to remain in desert places. Robertson notes the imperfect ἤρχοντο: people still came to him even in the wilderness. Ellicott and Clarke observe that the man's proclamation is a genuine irony: he meant to honour Christ but hampered the mission.

Calvin is measured: Christ bore the consequence of the man's enthusiasm without complaint, but the episode teaches that obedience to Christ's commands is better than the most zealous self-expression of gratitude. Gill notes that Jesus retired to desert places, but even there 'they came to him from every quarter.'
Wesley finds in the man's disobedience a sobering lesson about zeal without submission—even sincere gratitude must bow to Christ's explicit instruction.
